access.format: IIIF Presentation 2 metadata record with two sample images
notes: The two canvases show William Hunter's inscription and an opening text page; they have zero overlap with ff. 166r-v and are not a complete codex surrogate.

The manifest's license field points to the Glasgow home page rather than a recognized reuse license, while current Glasgow publication guidance requires advance permission. The sample images were therefore not separately cached or modeled as a reusable facsimile.

access.accessMethod: Email Archives and Special Collections with the shelfmark and a targeted research question. The current service can share up to 20 quick-reference snaps and can advise on fuller imaging or a virtual reading-room appointment.

access.provider: University of Glasgow Library, Archives and Special Collections

dateStatements.label: Preferred current scholarly date
dateStatements.notes: The current catalog retains the legacy Cent. XIV description but separately records Doyle's early-fifteenth-century hand assessment.

entities.description: The direct manuscript witness of CME302. The cataloged component begins at f. 166r line 26 and ends on f. 166v; the larger On Bloodletting text begins at f. 165v line 14. The codex is an early-fifteenth-century English medical volume, while its current catalog also preserves older fourteenth-century and original-binding descriptions alongside A. I. Doyle's later corrections.

holding.notes: Archives and Special Collections, University of Glasgow. The full institutional reference is GB 247 MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1).

entities.label: Glasgow University Library, MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1), ff. 166r-v

physicalDescription.dimensions: Binding 200 x 140 mm; folios about 190 x 130 mm; written space about 147-150 x 90-95 mm
physicalDescription.format: Bound codex; one column of 26 lines
notes: Black ink with alternating blue and red paragraph marks; gilt and colored initials elsewhere in the codex.
The ff. 165v-166v text is in a careful Gothic textura book hand.
The seventeenth-century marginal note 'Anthony July 13' appears on f. 166v.

rights.basis: The text is anonymously translated and attested in an early-fifteenth-century manuscript; this conclusion is limited to the historical text and not modern photography, transcription, or editorial matter.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English text on ff. 166r-v

access.accessMethod: Use Glasgow's publishing-request instructions and contact Archives and Special Collections before publishing manuscript images. Permission, acknowledgement, fees, and separate copyright clearance may apply.

access.format: Online instructions and application route
access.provider: University of Glasgow Library, Archives and Special Collections

access.accessMethod: Open WebHunter, choose Hunter 307, and use its three manuscript sections. CME302 is in the second section at f. 166r-v, JPEGs pag99-c.jpg and pag100-c.jpg. Deep Angular routes may return an error on direct reload, so begin at the home page if necessary.
access.entityLabel: WebHunter JPEG image sequences for MS Hunter 307
access.format: HTML viewer and JPEG images
notes: No local copy is recorded. The provider's exact copyright language prohibits copying the images without prior written consent, so systematic extraction was not performed.

The first 98 hu307p2 slots duplicate hu307p1 surfaces, and hu307p3 also assigns one full-folio JPEG to separate left/right transcription slots. Provider numpages counts must not be appended or summed as unique images.
access.provider: The Málaga Corpus of Late Middle English Scientific Prose (WebHunter)

rights.basis: The WebHunter copyright page gives a site-wide facsimile-image credit to Glasgow University Library and the Wellcome Library, says all rights are reserved, and requires prior written consent to use images or pictures. Hunter 307 is held by Glasgow, but the page does not separately allocate rights for this exact image sequence.
rights.component: WebHunter facsimile images of MS Hunter 307
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and applicable copyright law
notes: Public viewing is not redistribution permission. No systematic local cache was made.
Contact Glasgow Archives and Special Collections and the WebHunter rights holders before publishing or bulk copying these images.

entities.description: The Málaga Corpus WebHunter project publicly presents Hunter 307 JPEGs in three editorial viewer sequences. Provider count files report 329 hu307p1 slots, 100 hu307p2 slots, and 38 hu307p3 slots, but these are viewer/transcription positions rather than unique image objects or physical surfaces. The first 98 hu307p2 slots duplicate hu307p1 from f. 115r through f. 165v; only hu307p2 pag99-c.jpg and pag100-c.jpg add ff. 166r-v. In hu307p3, slots 0-21 are eleven left/right transcription pairs whose two slots reuse one byte-identical full-folio JPEG; slot 22 is f. 172v left, and slots 23-37 are distinct blank or end surfaces. CME302 is on pag99-c.jpg and pag100-c.jpg.
entities.label: WebHunter JPEG image sequences for MS Hunter 307
notes: No local image bundle was created because the provider's specific copyright page says the facsimile images are all rights reserved and may not be copied or used without prior written consent.

The site-wide image credit groups Glasgow University Library and the Wellcome Library; Hunter 307 is held by Glasgow, but WebHunter does not separately itemize exact image-rights allocation for this manuscript.
WebHunter's description says the codex lacks f. 161, while the viewer exposes f. 161r-v and Glasgow's current catalog reports missing ff. 147-148.

rights.basis: The WebHunter copyright page gives a site-wide facsimile-image credit to Glasgow University Library and the Wellcome Library, says all rights are reserved, and requires prior written consent to use images or pictures. Hunter 307 is held by Glasgow, but the page does not separately allocate rights for this exact image sequence.
rights.component: WebHunter facsimile images of MS Hunter 307
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and applicable copyright law
notes: Public viewing is not redistribution permission. No systematic local cache was made.

notes: The broad footer license and the more restrictive specific copyright wording conflict in practical scope. Obtain clarification before copying or republishing the transcription.

next_steps: Consult the complete current eVK2 records for v0034490000, v0057530000, v0088010000, and v0096920000.
Compare the opening, closing, and surrounding material in all four witnesses.
Keep the three British Library witnesses as cited collateral versions rather than ancestors until direct collation establishes their relationship.
openQuestions.question: Does VK 1841 represent an independently circulating garsing work, a version-specific component of On Bloodletting, or both under different witness boundaries?

next_steps: Request written permission from Glasgow Archives and Special Collections and ask which body controls the WebHunter derivatives.
Ask whether private bulk research storage, scholarly quotation, noncommercial repository display, and commercial print-on-demand reuse are treated differently.
If permission is granted, construct an overlap-aware full sequence rather than concatenating all three editorial segments.
notes: No image bundle was cached under the current express no-copy wording.
openQuestions.question: Will Glasgow and the WebHunter rights holders authorize a complete private research cache or publication/repository reuse of the Hunter 307 image sequence?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether legacy photography, microfilm, or restored digital objects cover the exact folios.
Request whole-codex or work-covering reproductions and item-level reuse terms if no public object exists.
Recheck the exact catalog records as post-cyberattack services are restored.
openQuestions.question: Do complete or work-covering digital surrogates exist for Royal MS 18 A VI, Sloane MS 965, or Sloane MS 3486 outside the current public catalog records?

next_steps: Establish the exact public release date of U-M CME302 and whether the article authors had access to it before publication.
Distinguish a raw digital transcript from a formally published semi-diplomatic scholarly edition in any future priority statement.
openQuestions.question: How should the 2023 article's claim to provide the first edition of the witness be reconciled with Schaffner's 2020 transcript and the U-M item's 2023 publication date?

next_steps: Consult the complete eVK2 v0096920000 record or obtain images of ff. 147v-148v.
Ask the British Library to confirm the exact terminal side when ordering photography.
Retain ff. 147v-148 without silently expanding it to 148r until direct evidence resolves the range.
openQuestions.question: Which side or extent of Sloane MS 3486, f. 148 completes the U-M XML's literal locator 'ff. 147v-48'?

reviewNotes: CME302 is scoped to the final line of f. 166r and f. 166v, not to the complete ff. 165v-166v On Bloodletting text.
Voigts's treatment of the Hunter passage within VK 0659.00 and of the three parallel passages as VK 1841.00 is preserved as a repertory-boundary problem rather than silently resolved.

The first 98 WebHunter hu307p2 viewer slots duplicate hu307p1 surfaces from f. 115r through f. 165v, and hu307p3 repeats full-folio JPEGs across paired left/right transcription slots; provider numpages values are viewer slots, not unique images or physical surfaces.

WebHunter's description says f. 161 is missing, but its own viewer exposes f. 161r-v; Glasgow's current catalog instead reports missing ff. 147-148. The conflict is retained rather than silently reconciled.

The current University of Glasgow IIIF catalog manifest has only two zero-overlap sample canvases. Its license field points to the Glasgow home page rather than a reuse license, and current publication guidance requires advance permission, so the samples remain catalog evidence and were not separately cached or modeled as a reusable facsimile.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is the pinned repository representation of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME302, a raw transcript made by Paul F. Schaffner in May 2020 and marked up in December 2020. It transcribes only Hunter 307, f. 166r line 26-f. 166v: the final garsing and ventosing component of the larger Middle English On Bloodletting beginning at f. 165v line 14. A 2023 article supplies a later semi-diplomatic edition, codicology, and dialect analysis, while three separately modeled plates reproduce ff. 165v-166v; no derivation from Schaffner's transcript is evidenced. WebHunter's JPEG facsimile and María Laura Esteban Segura's diplomatic transcription are also separate digital layers. Its first two viewer sequences overlap by 98 slots, and its third repeats full-folio images across left/right transcription slots, so published slot counts are not unique physical surfaces. CME302 maps to hu307p2 JPEGs pag99-c.jpg-pag100-c.jpg and text files page98.txt-page99.txt. Explicit WebHunter image terms prohibit copying without prior written consent, so no image bundle was cached. The complete scholarly article, public-domain 1908 Hunterian catalog, and current British Library digitisation price list were cached. The U-M XML also identifies collateral versions in Royal MS 18 A VI, Sloane MS 965, and Sloane MS 3486; these are supporting witnesses cited by the encoding, not ancestors of CME302. No public complete image surrogate was found in their current British Library records, but exact reproduction-order and permissions routes are recorded.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; Guy de Chauliac is the attributed author of the larger source text
catalogSummary.date: Translated after 1363 and attested by the early fifteenth century; the earliest surviving witness is unresolved between Hunter 307 and the broadly dated Sloane 965 bloodletting section
catalogSummary.region: England; the Hunter witness language is tentatively localized to the Midlands, probably Huntingdonshire
catalogSummary.title: Treatise on Garsing

catalogingSubject.label: Treatise on Garsing
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English prose component conventionally titled Treatise on Garsing, beginning with the final line of Hunter 307, f. 166r and continuing through f. 166v. It gives instructions for garsing (scarification) and ventosing (cupping). In the Hunter witness this component concludes the larger text identified by Esteban-Segura and Soriano-Jiménez as a Middle English On Bloodletting derived from Guy de Chauliac. Voigts's repertory treats the Hunter passage within VK 0659.00 but gives its three cited parallel versions separate VK 1841.00 entries. The component, its larger host work, Guy's Latin source tradition, four physical witnesses named by the U-M XML, Paul Schaffner's transcript, the later semi-diplomatic edition, and the digital representations remain distinct.

contentParts.label: U-M editorial framing
notes: This retained modern paratext is represented for item-boundary accounting but is not part of the medieval work, does not make the work multilingual, and does not make Schaffner its author.
contentParts.summary: Modern encoding-level title matter, Schaffner byline and May 2020 dateline, and an argument identifying the source locus, repertory boundary, and three collateral witnesses.

contentParts.label: Treatise on Garsing

locators.value: f. 166r, line 26-f. 166v; semi-diplomatic edition on printed pp. 117 and 119
Figures 14-15, printed pp. 116 and 118; Hunter 307, ff. 166r-v

notes: The work boundary is intellectual and repertorial; the manuscript does not supply a separate rubric at the opening.
contentParts.summary: The full cataloged prose component, from its instruction about hidden small veins through the closing practitioner testimonial.

assertion.notes: The lower bound is Guy's 1363 Chirurgia magna; the upper bound is the early-fifteenth-century Hunter witness. This brackets the translation rather than assigning an exact year.

dateStatements.display: After 1363 and by the early fifteenth century

scope.description: The Middle English translation represented by the Hunter passage.

assertion.notes: The lower bound follows Guy's 1363 source work rather than assigning Sloane 965 to a precise year within its broad catalog range. Hunter 307 supplies a secure terminus by the early fifteenth century, but neither witness is designated the confirmed earliest attestation.

dateStatements.display: After 1363 and by the early fifteenth century; earliest surviving witness unresolved

scope.description: The Hunter 307 passage is early fifteenth century, while the relevant Sloane 965 bloodletting section has the broader fourteenth-fifteenth-century date; the evidence does not establish which surviving version is earlier.

extent.description: One cataloged medieval prose component in one XML DIV1 and one body P, plus a separately modeled modern U-M editorial frame consisting of two heads, an opener/byline/dateline, and one argument paragraph. The work begins on the final line of Hunter 307, f. 166r and continues through f. 166v. The paratext has item_count 0 and is excluded from the intrinsic work count.

formStatements.description: Continuous instructional medical prose; editorial headings and the U-M argument do not change the work's form.

assertion.notes: The passage gives procedural directions for scarification and cupping and derives from Guy de Chauliac's surgical tradition.
Translation status is established for the larger On Bloodletting text; the exact Latin exemplar and component boundary remain unknown.

notes: The 2023 article calls its edition the first edition of the witness, although Schaffner made the U-M transcript in 2020 and the U-M item header dates public distribution to 2023. The record therefore avoids asserting an unqualified first-publication priority between the two 2023 appearances.
The article's Huntingdonshire finding describes the language of ff. 165v-166v; it is not treated as a secure place of translation or composition.
The Glasgow catalog's broad legacy date and binding description are preserved as attributed historical claims, not silently reconciled with Doyle's later assessment.

next_steps: Consult the complete current eVK2 records v0034490000, v0057530000, v0088010000, and v0096920000.
Compare the exact openings, closings, and surrounding text in Royal 18 A VI, Sloane 965, and Sloane 3486.
Retain the current component-plus-parent model until repertory or textual evidence resolves whether VK 1841 is an independent work or a portable section.
openQuestions.question: Should the Hunter garsing passage be cataloged as an independently circulating work/version, or only as the final component of On Bloodletting?

next_steps: Collate the Hunter text against critical editions and manuscripts of the phlebotomy material in Guy de Chauliac's Chirurgia magna.
Distinguish textual-source identification from identification of the translator's physical exemplar.
openQuestions.question: What exact Latin passage and physical exemplar underlie the Middle English translation represented in Hunter 307?

notes: No exact place of translation is established.

assertion.notes: The 2023 eLALME analysis places the witness language broadly in the Midlands and probably in Huntingdonshire. It is not transferred to the anonymous translator's biography or the work's composition event.

scope.description: The language of Hunter 307, ff. 165v-166v, including the cataloged final component.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English translator; Guy de Chauliac is the attributed author of the larger source text

assertion.notes: This catalog display is not a claim that Guy produced the Middle English wording or that the conventional garsing component had independent authorial status.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator; Guy de Chauliac is the attributed author of the larger source text

scope.description: Composite discovery display preserving the anonymous translation and the qualified attribution of its larger source text.

assertion.notes: The attribution to an anonymous translator is inherited from the larger ff. 165v-166v On Bloodletting text in which this component occurs.

scope.description: The Middle English passage conventionally cataloged as Treatise on Garsing.

assertion.notes: The 2023 study identifies ff. 165v-166v as Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting, from the Chirurgia magna. The U-M XML itself does not name Guy and treats the final component's repertory boundary as ambiguous.

scope.description: Authorship of the larger source text and corresponding source passage, not of the Middle English translation as such.

notes: Schaffner is a modern transcriber, not the medieval author.

scope.description: The raw manuscript transcript represented by the U-M CME item and repository XML.

assertion.notes: The component belongs to the larger On Bloodletting text, although its immediate instructions concern scarification and ventosing.
The passage maps procedures to pains and ailments of the eyes, head, jaw, mouth, teeth, neck, shoulders, arms, chest, back, buttocks, and skin.

summaries.value: A short Middle English medical prose passage explaining that small, hidden veins may be treated by garsing (scarification) or ventosing (cupping), then specifying body sites for treating pains and ailments of the eyes, head, mouth, neck, shoulders, limbs, chest, back, and skin. It ends with the practitioner's defense of the work and claim to have cured many patients. In Hunter 307 it is the final component of a larger text on bloodletting. The encoded item also retains modern U-M title matter, a Schaffner byline and date, and an editorial argument about its source and parallel witnesses.

CME302 begins at Hunter 307, f. 166r, line 26, not at the opening of On Bloodletting on f. 165v, line 14. The modern heading Treatise on Garsing and the repertory's separate treatment of parallel versions justify discovery as a component, while the parent relationship remains explicit.

assertion.notes: This is a modern conventional title; the manuscript passage has no independent title rubric.

titles.supplied_by: University of Michigan CME heading, following the MED stencil

titles.value: For garsyng and ventosyng it bifalliþ ofte tymes

work.summary: A short Middle English medical prose passage explaining that small, hidden veins may be treated by garsing (scarification) or ventosing (cupping), then specifying body sites for treating pains and ailments of the eyes, head, mouth, neck, shoulders, limbs, chest, back, and skin. It ends with the practitioner's defense of the work and claim to have cured many patients. In Hunter 307 it is the final component of a larger text on bloodletting. The encoded item also retains modern U-M title matter, a Schaffner byline and date, and an editorial argument about its source and parallel witnesses.
